input_path: data\papers\Mertens and Denil - 2025 - Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical twin system is a lab-scale gantry crane comprising a physical crane structure and its controller, designed as a cyber-physical system for industrial automation research. Key characteristics include reproducibility, public availability, and application in validating digital twin research areas such as continuous validation, fault detection, and system evolution (DarTwin).
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The digital twin provides services including optimal control for crane operations, historical data logging for system performance tracking, data visualization for real-time monitoring, continuous validation to ensure model accuracy against physical data, and fault detection mechanisms to identify and diagnose system anomalies. These services target researchers and educators for validation, optimization, and educational use cases.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Python-based gantrylib module for system components; Docker containerization of TimescaleDB (time-series database), Eclipse Mosquitto (MQTT broker), and Grafana (dashboard) for data storage, real-time messaging, and visualization services.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The system utilizes the C4 Model for architectural description, featuring a context diagram (Figure 2) and six container components: Database for data storage, Dashboard for runtime visualization, Trajectory Generator Service for path planning, Simulation Service for predictive modeling, Continuous Validation Service for real-time validation, and MQTT Broker for inter-service communication via publish-subscribe messaging.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin utilizes CAD models for geometric representation of the gantry crane structure and a kinematic model for dynamic motion simulation. A Simulation Service processes real-time sensor data to predict crane behavior, while a TimescaleDB database manages time-series data logging for trajectory analysis and historical record-keeping.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Data from the physical crane is collected by the Physical Crane Interface (Python program), which reads state parameters such as position and velocity from the hardware controllers. This data is continuously transmitted to the Logging Application, which writes it directly to a TimescaleDB database. The communication protocol between the physical crane and interface is unspecified. Transmission frequency is continuous but exact intervals are not provided. Data quality assurance, preprocessing steps, and integration mechanisms beyond direct database writes are not detailed in the document.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands for crane movement are generated by the Trajectory Generator Service and processed by the Control Application, which sends them to the Physical Crane Interface. The Continuous Validation Service compares executed trajectory measurements against simulations; discrepancies exceeding thresholds trigger operator notifications via the Dashboard. Specific command types, message formats, authentication mechanisms, feedback systems, conflict resolution, and command execution confirmation details are not provided in the document.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The system employs MQTT for inter-service communication between containers such as the Trajectory Generator, Simulation, and Control Applications. TimescaleDB is used for data storage. Network topology, bandwidth, latency requirements, reliability mechanisms, and security protocols are not detailed in the document.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: Deployed on a personal computer (on-premise) with Database, MQTT Broker, and Dashboard containers containerized using Docker. Python components are bundled into the gantrylib module and installed via pip. Computational and storage requirements, scalability mechanisms, and availability/reliability details beyond the described setup are not specified.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The Air Quality Management exemplar utilizes Model-Driven digital twin engineering methodologies, while the lab scale gantry crane employs the C4 Model for architecture visualization through context and container diagrams. Development processes across all exemplars are ad-hoc, using GitHub for version control and deployment, but specific quality assurance practices or evolution strategies are not detailed.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The Air Quality Management exemplar provides runtime visualization, simulation, and prediction capabilities for decision support. No specific insights or decision-making processes are described for the GreenhouseDT or Lab Scale Gantry Crane systems in the provided documents.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The Air Quality Management exemplar complies with the AutomationML standard for digital twin modeling, as indicated in the documentation. Other exemplars do not reference adherence to specific industry standards, utilizing ad-hoc approaches instead.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
# Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar



Joost Mertens

_Cosys-Lab & Flanders Make_
_University of Antwerp_
Antwerpen, Belgium
joost.mertens@uantwerpen.be


_**Abstract**_ **—The research topic of digital twins has attracted a**
**large amount of interest over the past decade. However, publicly**
**available exemplars remain scarce. In the interest of open and**
**reproducible science, in this exemplar paper we present a lab-**
**scale gantry crane and its digital twin. The exemplar comprises**
**both the physical and digital side of the twin system. The physical**
**side consists of the physical crane and its controller. The digital**
**side covers the CAD models and kinematic model of the crane,**
**and provides services for optimal control, historical data logging,**
**data visualization and continuous validation. We used this setup**
**as use case in several previous publications where its functionality**
**was validated. It is publicly available and only relies on other**
**freely available and commonly used software, this way we hope**
**it can be used for future research or education on the topic of**
**digital twins.**
_**Index Terms**_ **—exemplar, digital twin, open-source, cyber-**
**physical-system**


I. I NTRODUCTION

The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a
digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual
systems and services derived therefrom. During operation
data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the
twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual
system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both
physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it
mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3].
Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating
research results requires relevant use cases. Such research
results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster
data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar
in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the
following digital twin related research: continuous validation
methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a
notation for system evolution [7].
In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do
not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf
exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are
often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set
up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual
property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in
various domains would help reduce development overhead,
and additionally also also help support the generalizability of
research results.

Given the industry relevance of digital twins, education
is another field for which well described exemplars can be



Joachim Denil

block_1_judge.reasoning:
The extraction accurately describes the physical twin as a lab-scale gantry crane and its controller, identifies it as a cyber-physical system, and notes its characteristics of reproducibility and public availability. It correctly lists the specific research areas (continuous validation, fault detection, DarTwin) where the system is applied.

Supporting quotes:
- "lab-scale gantry crane and its digital twin"
- "physical side consists of the physical crane and its controller"
- "used as a demonstrator for the following digital twin related research: continuous validation methods, fault detection methods and DarTwin"

The description correctly identifies the core services provided by the digital twin, including optimal control, historical data logging, data visualization, and continuous validation. It also accurately identifies the target users as researchers and educators. The mention of fault detection is supported by the text's description of the continuous validation service and its use in research.

Supporting quotes:
- "services for optimal control, historical data logging, data visualization and continuous validation"
- "Trajectory Generator Service: a service that utilizes a kinematics model... to generate optimal movement trajectories"
- "used for future research or education"

The extraction precisely lists the specific tools and technologies used, including the Python module 'gantrylib', Docker containerization, and the specific software components (TimescaleDB, Eclipse Mosquitto, Grafana) along with their correct roles/functions (database, messaging, visualization). Supported by quotes: 'bundled all the Python C4 Containers in a Python module named gantrylib', 'Database, MQTT Broker and Dashboard C4 Containers are containerized with Docker', 'choice for TimescaleDB', 'ubiquitous and open-source Eclipse Mosquitto was used'.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses


2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X)
3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)























Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.


III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_

block_2_judge.reasoning: The description 'Not in Document' is correct as the source documents don't contain relevant information about temporal aspects of the Digital Twin including synchronization frequencies, time scales for different services, latency requirements, and temporal granularity needs. While the document mentions continuous logging and comparison, it doesn't provide specific technical temporal details.

The description 'Not in Document' is correct as the source documents don't contain relevant information about multiple digital twin instances, centralized vs decentralized architectures, coordination mechanisms between twins, and scope of responsibilities for each instance. The document only describes a single digital twin system for the crane.

The extraction is highly specific and directly supported by the source text. It correctly identifies the use of the C4 Model and accurately lists the six container components along with their specific functions, such as 'Trajectory Generator Service' and 'MQTT Broker'.

The description 'Not in Document' is correct as the source documents don't contain relevant information about integration with external systems including data exchange protocols, interoperability standards, dependencies on external systems, and integration patterns with other Digital Twins or enterprise systems. The document only mentions internal communication via MQTT and that the system is hosted on GitHub.

block_4_docs_retrieved:
III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_


Figure 2 shows the context diagram of the system. In
essence, it shows the high-level abstraction of what a digital
twin is, and two actors that interact with the system. The
operator is the actor that would operate and monitor this
system day to day, whilst the researcher can perform digital
twin research on it.
Figure 3 shows the container [6] diagram of the system. We
have limited ourselves to the most necessary relationships to
keep the diagram readable.
The gantry crane system, which can operate without digital
twin if needed, consists of five containers.
**Physical Crane:** the actual hardware of the crane.


5 [https://c4model.com/](https://c4model.com/)
6 Not a Docker container, a container is an abstraction in the C4 model for
something that must be there for the system to work.


Fig. 3. Container diagram of the system.



**Physical Crane Interface:** the interface to the physical
crane, a Python program connecting to the controllers of the

crane.

**Control Application:** a Python application that uses the
crane interface to control the crane.

**Logging Application:** a Python application that continuously logs the crane’s state to the database.
**Human Machine Interface:** a Python front-end for the
operator to configure and control the crane.
The digital twin consists of six containers. We’ll describe
them bottom up.

**Database:** a TimescaleDB database that contains all the data

created by the system and its various services.
**Dashboard:** a Grafana dashboard that visualizes data from

the database.

**Trajectory Generator Service:** a service that utilizes a
kinematics model of the crane to generate optimal movement
trajectories for the crane. Such optimal trajectories have no
residual swinging motion at the end.
**Simulation Service:** a service that implements a simulation
of the crane and it’s controllers. It can simulate for example
trajectories generated by the Trajectory Generator
**Continuous Validation Service:** a service that continuously
compares measurements of executed trajectories with simulations of those same trajectories. It calculates distance metrics
between them and compares the value with an acceptable
threshold. In case the threshold is breached operators are
notified via the dashboard and can take appropriate action.
**MQTT Broker:** an MQTT broker for signaling between
services.

Finally, we will not discuss the components of each of the
containers, but limit ourselves to the **Physical Crane** to give

block_4_judge.reasoning: Accurately describes the data flow from the Physical Crane Interface to the Logging Application and then to the TimescaleDB database, noting the continuous nature of the logging. It correctly identifies the interface as a Python program. However, it infers specific state parameters ('position and velocity') which are not explicitly listed in the text as logged data types (the text only mentions 'crane's state' and 'measurements of executed trajectories'), so the score is slightly reduced from 5.

Provides a highly specific and accurate description of the control loop. It correctly identifies the Trajectory Generator Service as the source of commands, the Control Application as the processor, and the Continuous Validation Service's role in comparing simulations and measurements. It also accurately describes the notification mechanism for threshold breaches.

Correctly identifies MQTT as the protocol for inter-service signaling and TimescaleDB for storage. It accurately reflects the provided architecture regarding communication between services and explicitly states that network topology and security details are not present in the text.

Provides a highly specific and accurate description of the deployment: running on a personal computer, using Docker for the Database, MQTT Broker, and Dashboard, and installing Python components via the gantrylib module and pip. It correctly notes that computational requirements and scalability details are not specified.

block_5_docs_retrieved:
_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses


2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X)
3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)























Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.
